perturbation.original_text: The Joint Venturers agree and declare that this association for the carrying on of a joint venture business operation does not, and is not intended to create a partnership, for either legal or United States income tax purposes, each Party recognizing that the other is willing and able to contribute capital, labor, and services for the operation of a successful joint venture business. Further, each Party elects under the authority of Section 761(a) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 (the "Code"), as amended and all successor statutes, to be excluded from the application of all of the provisions of Subchapter K of Chapter 1 of the Subtitle A of the Code, and the Parties agree that the election out of Subchapter K of Chapter 1 of Subtitle A of the Code shall, if necessary, be manifested by their execution and filing of all appropriate documentation.
perturbation.changed_text: The Joint Venturers agree and declare that this association for the carrying on of a joint venture business operation does not, and is not intended to create a partnership, for either legal or United States income tax purposes, each Party recognizing that the other is willing and able to contribute capital, labor, and services for the operation of a successful joint venture business. Further, each Party elects under the authority of Section 761(a) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 (the "Code"), as amended and all successor statutes, to be included within the application of all of the provisions of Subchapter K of Chapter 1 of the Subtitle A of the Code, and the Parties agree that the election into Subchapter K of Chapter 1 of Subtitle A of the Code shall, if necessary, be manifested by their execution and filing of all appropriate documentation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly elects *out* of Subchapter K of the Internal Revenue Code. The modified text elects *into* it, creating a direct contradiction. This affects how the joint venture is taxed, particularly regarding the distribution of profits and losses.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 761(a) allows certain unincorporated organizations to elect to be excluded from the application of Subchapter K, which governs the tax treatment of partnerships. The original agreement exercises this election. The modified text contradicts this by stating the parties elect *into* Subchapter K, which would subject them to partnership tax rules they explicitly intended to avoid.

Terms defined (a) Partnership For purposes of this subtitle, the term "partnership" includes a syndicate, group, pool, joint venture, or other unincorporated organization through or by means of which any business, financial operation, or venture is carried on, and which is not, within the meaning of this title, a corporation or a trust or estate. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 761 - Terms defined | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 761 - Terms defined U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) (a) Partnership For purposes of this subtitle, the term “ partnership ” includes a syndicate, group, pool, joint venture, or other unincorporated organization through or by means of which any business, financial operation, or venture is carried on, and which is not, within the meaning of this title, a corporation or a trust or estate. Under regulations the Secretary may, at the election of all the members of an unincorporated organization, exclude such organization from the application of all or part of this subchapter, if it is availed of— (1) for investment purposes only and not for the active conduct of a business, (2) for the joint production, extraction, or use of property, but not for the purpose of selling services or property produced or extracted, or (3) by dealers in securities for a short period for the purpose of underwriting, selling, or distributing a particular issue of securities, if the income of the members of the organization may be adequately determined without the computation of partnership taxable income. (b) Partner For purposes of this subtitle, the term “ partner ” means a member of a partnership . In the case of a capital interest in a partnership in which capital is a material income-producing factor, whether a person is a partner with respect to such interest shall be determined without regard to whether such interest was derived by gift from any other person. (c) Partnership agreement For purposes of this subchapter, a partnership agreement includes any modifications of the partnership agreement made prior to, or at, the time prescribed by law for the filing of the partnership return for the taxable year (not including extensions) which are agreed to by all the partners, or which are adopted in such other m
perturbation.justification: The original text states an election *out* of Subchapter K, while the changed text states an election *into* it. This directly contradicts the initially intended tax treatment. 26 U.S. Code § 761(a), as referenced in both the Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 2, allows unincorporated organizations to elect to be excluded from Subchapter K. The modification reverses this election, subjecting the joint venture to partnership tax rules they explicitly sought to avoid. This is a significant and direct contradiction, undermining the original intent of the agreement regarding tax treatment as specified by the Internal Revenue Code.

perturbation.original_text: Distributions may only be made if after any distribution is made, the Joint Venture assets are in excess of all liabilities of the Joint Venture.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributions may only be made if the Joint Venture's board of directors approves the distribution.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires that distributions only be made when assets exceed liabilities which reflects solvency requirements. The modified text allows distributions based solely on board approval, potentially violating Pennsylvania's laws regarding distributions that could render the company insolvent.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law, specifically 15 Pa.C.S. § 1551, imposes restrictions on distributions that would render a corporation insolvent. The modified text removes the solvency requirement and relies solely on board approval, potentially violating this law if the board approves a distribution that impairs the corporation's ability to pay its debts as they become due.

Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 15 - CORPORATIONS AND UNINCORPORATED ASSOCIATIONS Chapter 15 - Corporate Powers, Duties and Safeguards Section 1551 - Distributions to shareholders Universal Citation: 15 PA Cons Stat § 1551 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 1551. Distributions to shareholders. (a) General rule.-- Unless otherwise restricted in the bylaws, the board of directors may authorize and a business corporation may make distributions. A provision in the articles setting forth a par value for any authorized shares or class or series of shares shall not restrict the ability of a corporation to make distributions. (b) Limitation.-- A distribution, including a distribution under Subchapter F (relating to voluntary dissolution and winding up) or H (relating to postdissolution provision for liabilities) of Chapter 19, may not be made if, after giving effect thereto: (1) the corporation would be unable to pay its debts as they become due in the usual course of its business; or (2) the total assets of the corporation would be less than the sum of its total liabilities plus (unless otherwise provided in the articles) the amount that would be needed, if the corporation were to be dissolved at the time as of which the distribution is measured, to satisfy the preferential rights upon dissolution of shareholders whose preferential rights are superior to those receiving the distribution. (c) Valuation.-- The board of directors may base its determination that a distribution is not prohibited under subsec
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a solvency requirement with board approval for distributions, creating a potential contradiction with Pennsylvania law. 15 Pa.C.S. § 1551(b)(1) states that a distribution may not be made if "the corporation would be unable to pay its debts as they become due in the usual course of its business." The original text ensures distributions are tied to the company's financial health (assets exceeding liabilities), whereas the changed text allows distributions based solely on board discretion, disregarding the solvency requirements outlined in the provided snippet from 15 Pa.C.S. § 1551. This creates a pathway for distributions that could leave the corporation unable to pay its debts, directly contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Joint Venturers declare that in entering into this Agreement, they have contracted with reference to the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, and the construction and interpretation of the terms and provisions of this Agreement shall be interpreted and construed under the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, except in such cases and to such extent as the laws of another jurisdiction shall necessarily control.
perturbation.changed_text: The Joint Venturers declare that in entering into this Agreement, they have contracted with reference to the laws of the State of Delaware, and the construction and interpretation of the terms and provisions of this Agreement shall be interpreted and construed under the laws of the State of Delaware, except in such cases and to such extent as the laws of another jurisdiction shall necessarily control.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies Pennsylvania law as governing the contract's interpretation. The modified version changes this to Delaware law. Because contract law varies between states, this creates uncertainty regarding which state's laws apply, especially if the business is physically located and operating primarily in Pennsylvania. This has implications on interpretation of breach of contract, force majeure, and other critical terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly violating a specific statute, the change undermines Pennsylvania's choice-of-law principles, which generally respect contractual agreements on governing law *if* the chosen jurisdiction has a reasonable relationship to the parties or the transaction (See also *PHL Variable Ins. Co. v. Price Dawe 2006 Ins. Tr.*, 28 A.3d 779 (Pa. 2011). Shifting the governing law to Delaware without a clear connection to that state raises questions about the enforceability of this clause, potentially leading a Pennsylvania court to disregard it and apply Pennsylvania law based on the location of the joint venture's operations.
perturbation.justification: The modification in the contract changes the governing law from Pennsylvania to Delaware. The Law Explanation states that Pennsylvania choice-of-law principles respect contractual agreements on governing law if the chosen jurisdiction has a reasonable relationship to the parties or the transaction. Changing to Delaware law without a clear connection raises questions about enforceability, as the court may disregard it and apply Pennsylvania law. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.
